According to the investigation, Gensler's government-issued phone stopped syncing with the SEC's device management system on 6 July 2023.

The SEC deleted a full year of Gary Gensler's communications at the peak of his campaign against crypto.

The FDIC tried to secretly cut off an entire lawful industry from the banking system and buried the evidence.

The US government doesn't get to operate like this. Unless the… pic.twitter.com/jpOTjUVcAa

— Paul Grewal (@iampaulgrewal) July 22, 2026

Although he continued using the device for 62 days, neither he nor SEC staff noticed it had been flagged as inactive.

The situation became more serious after the SEC introduced a new IT policy on 10 August 2023, automatically wiping any government device that had failed to connect to the system for 45 days, assuming it had been lost or stolen.

When Gensler later realised his SEC applications had disappeared, staff attempted to restore access.

Instead, the recovery process triggered a factory reset that permanently erased messages covering the period from 18 October 2022 to September 2023.

The inspector general concluded the loss was "avoidable", blaming missed system alerts, poor coordination with the agency's device vendor and the absence of recent backups.

🙈🙊🙉 GARY GENSLER'S AVOIDABLE ERROR

Gary Gensler's former SEC Chairman deleted text messages avoidable error 🤔

Interesting dates 📅
October 18, 2022, to September 6, 2023.

"The report contains five recommendations that should further
strengthen the SEC's management of… pic.twitter.com/2Yyyr2sxdl

— Stephanie (@stephmase22) July 16, 2026

Even so, investigators recovered about 1,500 related text messages from other officials' devices.

Around 38% of those conversations were considered "mission-related", including discussions on the timing of enforcement actions against crypto trading platforms.

SEC Agrees To Tighten Record Retention Rules

As part of the settlement, Grewal said the SEC agreed to overhaul its record-retention practices, including ending automatic device wiping for senior officials.

The changes are consistent with reforms the regulator had already begun implementing after the inspector general's findings were published in September 2025.

The SEC has also disabled text messaging on most government-issued devices, notified the National Archives about the missing records and committed to all five recommendations made by the watchdog.

Among them is a requirement for management approval before any future factory reset can be carried out.

In an opinion piece published by The Wall Street Journal, Grewal criticised the regulator's handling of the incident.

"The agency tasked with policing corporate record-keeping somehow lost reams of its own text messages between Mr. Gensler and other officials during the most intense period of the anti-crypto campaign."

He also argued that the SEC had failed to meet the same standards it imposed on the private sector, saying the regulator insisted "everybody should play by the same rules" while making the very mistake it had penalised financial firms for over the years.

Leadership Changes Follow A Softer SEC Approach

The settlement arrives after the SEC adopted a more crypto-friendly direction under Chair Paul Atkins.

The agency dropped several major enforcement actions against crypto firms during 2025, including its headline case against Coinbase, after Acting Chair Mark Uyeda dismissed the lawsuit before Atkins took over permanently.

Coinbase is also preparing for a leadership transition within its legal team.

Grewal, who has served as the company's Chief Legal Officer since 2020, will move into an advisory role from 31 July 2026.

Coinbase legal vice presidents Molly Abraham and Ryan VanGrack are expected to become general counsel and vice chair, respectively.

After 6 years I’m leaving @Coinbase. I’ll be transitioning to an advisory role at the end of the month and continue my service on the Board of Coinbase National Trust Company. I will be a Coinbase ally for life and am grateful to @brian_armstrong, @emilemc and the Coinbase board…

— Paul Grewal (@iampaulgrewal) July 9, 2026

data.title: It's time to pass the Clarity Act.
data.description:
Author: Chris Dixon Source: a16zcrypto Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Every generation has the opportunity to upgrade its infrastructure.

In the 1990s, the internet was that opportunity. Policymakers worried it would disrupt existing industries and bring new risks. But instead of forcing this new technology into outdated regulatory frameworks, legislators created rules that protected consumers while allowing innovation to flourish. As a result, the United States experienced one of the greatest periods of economic growth in history.

Today, blockchain networks present similar opportunities. Stablecoins are making payments faster and cheaper; tokenization is modernizing capital markets. Both rely on blockchain infrastructure.

These technologies will continue to evolve regardless of whether Congress acts. The question is: should the United States set the standards, or should it relinquish this position?

The GENIUS Act Proves: Clear Rules Work. The GENIUS Act demonstrates what thoughtful policymaking can achieve. Stablecoins are digital dollars, circulating on the internet as easily as bits. Sending $200 from the US to Colombia through traditional channels can incur fees exceeding $12 and take several days. With stablecoins, the same transfer costs only cents and arrives in seconds. For years, regulatory uncertainty has hindered the adoption of stablecoins. The GENIUS Act changed this—eliminating ambiguity and unlocking growth potential by establishing reserve requirements and an issuer framework. The results speak for themselves: the stablecoin market has reached approximately $315 billion, growing by over 50% year-on-year, and dollar-backed tokens are becoming one of the fastest-growing channels for the circulation of US currency overseas. According to Visa, stablecoins have processed $100 trillion in transactions over the past twelve months. Large institutions such as JPMorgan Chase, Citigroup, Visa, Mastercard, and BlackRock are deeply integrating into blockchain infrastructure. More importantly, the US dollar is winning. Millions of people worldwide who previously lacked easy access to traditional US dollar accounts can now hold and trade digital dollars through software wallets. Clear rules help anchor this new system to US standards and regulations, rather than alternatives from competitors. GENIUS has proven that wise regulation expands, rather than shrinks, the market. But it only solves part of the problem. Blockchain networks still lack rules. By market capitalization, stablecoins represent less than 15% of the crypto market, but the remaining 85% of the underlying blockchain networks they rely on still operate without a coherent federal framework. This is akin to regulating smartphones while ignoring cellular networks. The CLARITY Act aims to address this issue. Its core principle is to provide clear rules for blockchain networks, defining the regulatory responsibilities of all parties in the digital asset market. It creates incentives for transparency, risk management, and competition under a common set of standards. By empowering regulators to oversee intermediaries using proven principles from traditional finance—proper custody, segregation of client assets, and adequate disclosure—it prevents another FTX-style disaster. It also opens the door to the next wave of institutional adoption. Large institutions are already taking action: BlackRock has launched a tokenized fund; JPMorgan Chase is building a blockchain-based payment system; and the DTCC, with $114 trillion in assets under custody, is preparing to expand tokenized securities on the Canton Network. CLARITY removes barriers to entry, providing a clear path for compliant participation in traditional finance. This benefits not only the crypto industry but also consumers, investors, and the long-term competitiveness of the US capital markets. The Choice Before Us History shows that open, neutral platforms governed by clear rules create the greatest value. The internet succeeded because entrepreneurs knew the rules—they could build products, attract capital, and compete on merit, not on guesswork about regulation. Blockchain networks deserve the same opportunity. "Regulation vs. Innovation" is a false proposition. GENIUS has already disproven it: in the second half of 2025—after GENIUS is signed into law—more than $13 billion flowed into crypto startups, almost double the $6.9 billion in the first half of that year (before GENIUS was signed). Meanwhile, forecasts show the tokenized asset market will grow 100-fold in the coming years. The real choice lies between certainty and uncertainty: the US can establish leadership and build the future domestically, or it can watch it be built elsewhere. No law is perfect, and the CLARITY Act is no exception. The reality is that the crypto space currently lacks any consumer protections. CLARITY will establish those protections. Like any bill, no one can get everything they want, but the version released today will push the industry forward. It represents months of bipartisan negotiations and significant compromises made by the industry. No matter how imperfect, the CLARITY Act is far better than continuing without any safeguards. Congress's decisions this year will determine where the next era of financial infrastructure will flourish and who will write the rules. If the CLARITY Act passes, the U.S. will once again lead the way, just as it did with the commercial internet. Without action, innovation will migrate elsewhere, operating within frameworks set by others.
